Analytic Philosophy of Mind

P. F. Strawson & Galen Strawson

1919 – 2006 / b. 1952

Persons, subjectivity, and the conceptual grammar of minded agency.

By “Strawson” I am treating the topic in the broadest useful sense: P. F. Strawson for the concept of the person, the grammar of mental ascription, and the anti-Cartesian account of the soul; Galen Strawson for consciousness, the self, materialism, panpsychism, and the intrinsic nature of the physical. The two are not a single doctrine, but they can be reconstructed into a powerful composite philosophy of mind: P. F. Strawson gives the public, embodied, interpersonal level of the mind; Galen Strawson gives the metaphysical and phenomenological level of experience itself.

The resulting view is neither Cartesian soul-dualism nor eliminative materialism. It says, roughly: the bearer of mind in our ordinary and philosophical scheme is the embodied person; consciousness is an undeniable concrete reality; the physical must be understood broadly enough to include experience; the “soul,” if retained, should not be conceived as a detachable immaterial ego but as the person’s irreducible experiential and personal life; and theology must take embodiment, divine consciousness, resurrection, creation, and panpsychism seriously rather than treating them as side issues. P. F. Strawson’s Individuals makes bodies and persons basic particulars in our conceptual scheme, while the “Persons” chapter turns on why states of consciousness are ascribed at all, and why they are ascribed to the same individuals to whom corporeal characteristics are ascribed. Galen Strawson’s “Realistic Monism” begins from the premise that experience is the most certain concrete phenomenon and argues that a real physicalism must include it rather than explain it away.

1. P. F. Strawson: the person before the soul

P. F. Strawson’s method is “descriptive metaphysics”: not the invention of a new metaphysical picture, but the description of the deep structure of our actual thought about the world. He contrasts this with “revisionary metaphysics,” which proposes a different structure. The point matters because Strawson’s theory of mind is not first an empirical psychology or a speculative ontology of mental substance; it is an account of the categorial framework within which we identify, reidentify, and predicate things of ourselves and others. In that framework, material bodies and persons are the basic or fundamental particulars.

This is the crucial anti-Cartesian move. For P. F. Strawson, the concept of a person is not built by first having the concept of a body, then the concept of a private mind or soul, and then joining them. The person is a primitive kind of subject: one and the same individual can be the subject of predicates ascribing consciousness and predicates ascribing bodily characteristics. In a standard formulation of the view, a person is a type of entity to which both predicates ascribing states of consciousness and predicates ascribing corporeal characteristics are applicable to a single individual of that type.

This means that P. F. Strawson rejects both of the main simplifications. The Cartesian view treats the real subject as a pure ego or immaterial soul, with the body as an associated object. The “no-ownership” view tries to avoid the ego by saying, in effect, that experiences are not really owned by any subject, or that ownership is just a misleading feature of language. Strawson’s argument is that the no-ownership theorist cannot even state the relevant facts without reintroducing “my” experiences or the experiences of a certain person; meanwhile the Cartesian pure ego cannot explain how distinct subjects of experience are identifiable at all.

The deeper thesis is that self-ascription and other-ascription are internally connected. I cannot possess the concept of my own experiences as mine in a purely private way, independently of the possibility of ascribing experiences to others. This does not make consciousness unreal or merely behavioral. It means that the concept of consciousness is anchored in the concept of persons, and persons are publicly identifiable embodied beings. The mind is therefore not a hidden object behind the body; nor is the body merely a container for the mind. The person is the primary subject.

This has immediate consequences for the soul. If “soul” means a pure private owner of experiences, metaphysically prior to the person, P. F. Strawson’s theory is hostile to it. But if “soul” means the person considered under the aspect of conscious, moral, and spiritual life, then Strawson’s theory does not eliminate soul-talk. It relocates it. The soul is not the hidden thing to which the person is attached; the person is the basic subject, and soul-language must answer to that embodied-personal unity.

2. Disembodiment, survival, and God as “person”

P. F. Strawson is often read as making disembodied personhood difficult but not simply meaningless. A useful theological reading of Individuals notes that Strawson allows the bare intelligibility of a disembodied consciousness, but only as a logically secondary case: the disembodied consciousness would be the continuing consciousness of a former person, not the primary model from which personhood is understood.

This is theologically important. It means that a doctrine of post-mortem survival cannot simply begin with a separable soul as the paradigm of personhood. The concept of a disembodied survivor depends on the concept of an embodied person whose life is continued, preserved, resurrected, remembered, or reconstituted. In Christian terms, this makes bodily resurrection or hylomorphic anthropology more Strawsonian than a crude “ghost survives the machine” picture.

The same issue arises for God. A line of philosophy of religion has used Strawson-like considerations to argue that “incorporeal person” is semantically problematic, because ordinary person-language is learned and applied with reference to embodied beings. Neil Manson’s discussion of God as an incorporeal person presents this objection and then argues that it need not defeat theism, because religious and philosophical terms can acquire technical or theoretical senses; one may also argue that divine knowing and acting in the material world give some analogical grip on person-predicates.

A rigorous Strawsonian theology therefore should not say too quickly, “God is a person in exactly the same sense as a human being.” Nor should it say, “God cannot be personal because God is not embodied.” The better conclusion is that divine personhood is not univocal with human personhood. It is either analogical, technical, or embedded in a wider doctrine of divine mind, agency, incarnation, and creation. Strawson forces theology to clarify how far human person-language can be extended beyond its embodied home.

3. P. F. Strawson’s moral psychology: mind as interpersonal

P. F. Strawson’s “Freedom and Resentment” should be included in any full philosophy of mind extracted from him. Its importance is not merely about free will. It adds a social and affective layer to the concept of person. Strawson focuses on “participant reactive attitudes” such as resentment and gratitude, which arise in response to how we take others to regard us. He argues that these attitudes are central to human life and cannot be globally suspended toward normal people merely because of determinism. Moral attitudes, blame, praise, and responsibility are rooted in interpersonal life, not in an abstract metaphysical proof of contra-causal freedom.

This matters for the soul because it blocks another distortion: the idea that the soul is primarily an inner spectator. For P. F. Strawson, the person is not only a bearer of experiences but also a participant in a moral community. The mind is not merely consciousness; it is also responsiveness, accountability, susceptibility to resentment and gratitude, and participation in practices of excuse, blame, forgiveness, and recognition.

A Strawsonian theology of the soul would therefore be personalist and relational. Confession, guilt, forgiveness, prayer, resentment, gratitude, and moral repair are not psychological decorations added to an independently defined soul. They are part of what it is for persons to be persons.

4. Galen Strawson: consciousness as the fundamental datum

Galen Strawson begins from a different point. Where P. F. Strawson begins from the conceptual scheme of persons and bodies, Galen begins from the reality of experience. In “Realistic Monism,” he defines physicalism as the view that every real concrete phenomenon in the universe is physical, and he immediately adds that mental goings-on are concrete phenomena. A “real” physicalist cannot deny the existence of experience, because experience is more certain than anything else.

This is not dualism. Galen’s target is a narrowed physicalism that tacitly defines the physical as whatever is describable in the vocabulary of physics as currently understood. He often attacks what he elsewhere calls the mistake of confusing physicalism with “physicsalism”: the idea that physics gives us the complete nature of the physical. His position is closer to Russellian monism: physics gives structural, mathematical, causal, and relational knowledge, but not the full intrinsic nature of concrete reality. The intrinsic nature of at least some concrete reality is known directly in experience. The French Klesis translation of his “Qu’est-ce que le matérialisme ?” makes this Russellian point especially clearly, stressing that physics does not tell us the intrinsic character of matter except where we know it in conscious experience.

In a 2026 Journal of Consciousness Studies article, Galen states the thesis even more starkly: consciousness is not a mystery in the sense often claimed; we know what it is because we have it, and it is the only concrete reality whose qualitative nature we know for certain. He also argues that physicalism is compatible with panpsychism and that panpsychist physicalism is the most plausible, or least implausible, account of concrete reality.

This yields a strong anti-eliminativist principle: any metaphysics that denies the reality of experience has disqualified itself. It is not being hard-headed or scientific; it is refusing the datum without which science, thought, and evidence have no lived reality.

5. Why Galen Strawson thinks physicalism entails panpsychism

Galen’s most famous argument is that real physicalism entails panpsychism or panexperientialism. The reasoning is compressed but powerful.

First, experience is real. Second, if physicalism is true, experience is physical. Third, experience cannot intelligibly emerge from wholly non-experiential being by brute emergence. For Galen, genuine emergence requires that the emergent feature be intelligibly traceable to what it emerges from; otherwise “emergence” is just a label for magic. Fourth, therefore, if experience exists in a physical world, the physical cannot be wholly non-experiential at the fundamental level. Some form of experientiality must already be involved in the nature of matter or energy.

This is not the claim that electrons think, deliberate, imagine, or pray. It is not the claim that tables and stones are conscious subjects in the way animals are. Galen explicitly says panpsychism need not hold that stones and tables are subjects of experience. The more careful thesis is that the ultimate constituents of physical reality are experience-involving, and that some organized composites, such as animals and perhaps living cells, may be subjects in a stronger sense.

This produces a radically expanded philosophy of mind. Mind is not an inexplicable latecomer to a dead universe. Nor is it an immaterial substance added to matter. Experience is part of what the physical is, once the physical is understood realistically rather than through a restricted scientific image.

6. The subject of experience: thin selves, “sesmets,” and the minimal soul

Galen Strawson does not stop with experience. He insists that experience is impossible without an experiencer, a subject of experience. But his subject is not the Cartesian soul. It is “thin”: there cannot be a subject of experience at a time unless some experience exists for it at that time. He introduces the term “sesmet” for a subject of experience considered as a single mental thing. A sesmet is not necessarily a long-lived person, not necessarily a human being, and not necessarily a substance in the classical soul sense.

This is one of the most important points for extracting a philosophy of the soul. Galen gives us something soul-like but not a traditional soul: a real subject of experience, materially or physically conceived, inseparable from occurrent experience, possibly short-lived, and not identical with the whole human being. His Oxford chapter “The Self” explicitly aims to give “the self,” “I,” and “subject of experience” a clear meaning within a wholly materialist framework, without taking them to refer simply to the whole human organism.

Spanish-language reception helps sharpen the issue. A recent Spanish discussion of agency and the philosophy of mind presents Galen’s “sujeto delgado” or thin subject as a phenomenological starting point that becomes the basis for a minimal concept of the self, while also criticizing Strawson for reifying the self even after rejecting long-term autobiographical persistence.

The criticism is serious. Galen wants to avoid the Cartesian ego, but he also wants to say that there really are subjects. P. F. Strawson might worry that this reintroduces something too close to the inner object. Galen’s reply would be that the subject is not an object behind experience; it is the living unity of experience itself, considered from the inside. The unresolved question is whether that is enough to avoid both no-ownership and Cartesianism.

7. The combination problem

Galen’s panpsychism faces the standard combination problem: how do many small subjects or experiential ultimates compose a larger unified subject, such as a human conscious subject? He acknowledges the difficulty directly. An experience of mine may be composed of many smaller experiences belonging to numerically distinct subjects, but I do not experience myself as many subjects. The idea that one subject can be constituted by many other distinct subjects is, as he says, famously difficult.

This is the central metaphysical pressure point in Galen’s system. P. F. Strawson gives us a public concept of the person as a unified subject of mental and corporeal predicates. Galen gives us a fundamental metaphysics of experiential ultimates and thin subjects. But the bridge between the micro-subjective and the human personal subject remains incomplete. A full Strawsonian philosophy of mind must therefore distinguish three levels:

The fundamental level: experiential ultimates or sesmets.

The animal-organismic level: brains, bodies, living systems, and perhaps cells as organized loci of experience.

The personal level: publicly identifiable persons who speak, act, remember, respond, and are morally addressed.

The system is strongest when it refuses to collapse these levels. It is weakest where it cannot explain how they compose.

8. Galen Strawson and theology: not religion, but not irrelevant

Galen Strawson’s panpsychism is not itself a theology. He even says that if one had to posit a universe-wide sesmet, that would have no more to do with religion than the view that there is a single universe. This is a useful warning against easy identifications of panpsychism with God, cosmic consciousness, or pantheism.

At the same time, theology cannot ignore Galen’s position. If experience is fundamental and perhaps ubiquitous, then the created order is not metaphysically dead. This has implications for doctrines of creation, incarnation, ecology, divine action, and the spiritual significance of nature.

The French Klesis translation is especially valuable here because it preserves Galen’s theological asides. He notes that some materialists who believe in God may formulate materialism as the claim that everything in the universe except God is material, while others exclude God from the universe; he also notes the Spinozistic option of identifying God and the universe. These comments show that Galen’s materialism is not automatically a crude atheistic thesis. It is primarily a thesis about concrete reality and the nature of the physical; theological interpretations require additional commitments.

In the same French essay, Galen says that resolving the mind-body problem requires two things: direct knowledge of the reality of one’s current stream of consciousness and knowledge, very far from reasonable doubt, that this reality is neural, bodily, or physical. This is a strikingly non-dualist, non-eliminativist formula: consciousness is not something over and above the body, but neither is it something that can be redescribed away in third-person terms.

9. The wider theological field

Recent theological work confirms that the mind-body problem is not peripheral to theology. Charles Taliaferro’s St Andrews Encyclopaedia of Theology article states that mind and consciousness are philosophically and theologically significant, especially for Christian views of human nature and God, and that Christian anthropology includes dualism, hylomorphism, materialism, and alternatives such as panpsychism. It also emphasizes that biblical and theological claims about God presuppose divine awareness, intention, agency, prayer, moral evaluation, incarnation, redemption, and judgment.

This context matters because a Strawsonian philosophy of mind cannot treat theology as an optional ornament. Theological claims about God’s mind, the soul, resurrection, incarnation, sin, prayer, and moral responsibility all depend on some account of consciousness, personhood, embodiment, and agency.

The theological panpsychism debate is especially relevant to Galen. Joanna Leidenhag argues that a Christian theologian can affirm panpsychism without becoming a process theologian by accepting mental realism, denying psychophysical reductionism, affirming fundamental monism, denying brute emergence, and then preserving substance ontology and creation ex nihilo. This is very close to Galen’s anti-reductionist and anti-brute-emergence argument, but with explicit Christian constraints added.

German theological discussion has also taken this route seriously. Brüntrup and Jaskolla’s “Panpsychismus und Handeln Gottes” explicitly examines how a panpsychist ontology of mind affects the concept of divine action and the God-world relation, motivated in part by Chalmers and Galen Strawson. They argue that a panpsychist worldview can permit an adequate account of the God-world relation and a substantive concept of divine action.

There are also cautions. A recent Theology Commons discussion distinguishes non-naturalistic and naturalistic panpsychisms, warning that some non-naturalistic forms may collapse the Creator-creation distinction. That is exactly the danger a Christian Strawsonian must avoid. The safest theological reconstruction is not “God is the consciousness of matter” or “the universe-wide subject is God,” but rather: consciousness is a created feature of the physical order, perhaps fundamental and widespread, while God remains the creator and sustainer of that order.

Panpsychism also intersects with ecological and incarnational theology. Cornel du Toit links panpsychism, pan-consciousness, ecological awareness, deep incarnation, and the non-human turn, noting that panpsychism extends consciousness or experience beyond the human and into nature. This does not follow automatically from Galen, but it is a serious theological development of the terrain his metaphysics opens.

10. The extracted Strawsonian philosophy of mind

The most coherent reconstruction has ten theses.

First, experience is real and non-negotiable. Any theory that denies consciousness or treats it as an illusion is not a serious philosophy of mind. Galen’s first datum is that experience exists; P. F. Strawson’s corresponding datum is that states of consciousness are actually ascribed to persons.

Second, the person is conceptually prior to the private soul. We do not first identify a pure ego and then attach a body to it. We identify persons as embodied subjects of both mental and corporeal predicates. This gives the philosophy of mind an anti-Cartesian structure.

Third, the body is not merely external to mind. The body is not a prison, shell, or accidental vehicle. It is part of the primary identification of persons. This does not reduce mind to body; it rejects the assumption that mind and body are two separately intelligible items that must later be glued together.

Fourth, the self has two legitimate levels. At the personal level, the self is the embodied human person, the subject of speech, action, memory, responsibility, and interpersonal recognition. At the phenomenological-metaphysical level, the self is the thin subject of experience, the occurrent experiential unity Galen calls a sesmet. Confusing these levels produces either Cartesianism or eliminativism.

Fifth, the soul should be reconstructed, not simply affirmed or denied. A Cartesian soul-substance is not supported by the Strawsonian picture. But a Strawsonian “soul” can mean the embodied person considered as an irreducible subject of conscious, moral, and spiritual life. In a Galenian register, it can also mean the minimal subjectivity inseparable from experience. In a theological register, it may be closer to hylomorphic “living form” or personal life before God than to an immaterial ghost.

Sixth, the physical must be enlarged. Physicalism is not the doctrine that reality is exhausted by the vocabulary of present physics. It is the doctrine that every real concrete phenomenon is physical; since experience is real, experience must be physical if physicalism is true. The real question is what the physical is intrinsically.

Seventh, brute emergence is not an explanation. Consciousness cannot arise from wholly non-conscious reality by mere declaration. If matter is genuinely physical and consciousness is genuinely real, then the physical must have an experiential or proto-experiential nature at some level. This is the argument for panpsychist physicalism.

Eighth, subjectivity is not necessarily human, but personhood is not automatically universal. Galen’s panpsychism may imply many subjects of experience, perhaps including micro-subjects and possibly living cells, but it does not require tables and stones to be subjects in the personal sense. P. F. Strawson’s person remains a richer category involving bodily, psychological, linguistic, social, and moral predicates.

Ninth, moral mind is interpersonal. The mind is not exhausted by qualia. Persons live in reactive attitudes: resentment, gratitude, blame, excuse, forgiveness, and responsibility. This is indispensable for any theological anthropology because sin, repentance, grace, and reconciliation concern persons, not bare streams of consciousness.

Tenth, theology must be disciplined by embodiment and creation. God’s consciousness cannot simply be modeled on human embodied consciousness, but neither can theology dispense with divine mind. Human survival cannot be modeled primarily on a free-floating soul, but resurrection or divine preservation of persons remains conceptually richer. Panpsychism can support a more enchanted doctrine of creation, but only if the Creator-creation distinction is preserved.

11. Major tensions and unresolved problems

The first major tension is between P. F. Strawson’s public person and Galen Strawson’s thin subject. P. F. argues against the pure ego and makes persons basic. Galen reintroduces a real subject of experience, though not as a Cartesian ego. The reconciliation is possible but delicate: the thin subject is not the whole person, and the whole person is not reducible to the thin subject. A comprehensive Strawsonian philosophy of mind must be explicitly two-level.

The second problem is composition. Galen’s panpsychism needs an account of how micro-experiential subjects compose macro-experiential subjects. Without such an account, the human person remains metaphysically underexplained from below, even if P. F. Strawson has described its conceptual role from above.

The third problem is individuation. P. F. Strawson’s persons are identified in a public spatiotemporal framework. Galen’s sesmets may be brief, overlapping, or compositionally complex. The theory needs principled criteria for when there is one subject rather than many, especially in brains, split-brain cases, animal consciousness, artificial systems, and possible group minds.

The fourth problem is theological survival. P. F. Strawson makes disembodiment secondary; Galen’s thin subjects are not naturally long-lived souls. A doctrine of personal immortality therefore cannot simply be extracted from Strawson. It needs additional theological premises: resurrection, divine preservation, re-embodiment, or a hylomorphic account of personal continuity.

The fifth problem is divine personhood. Strawson’s person-concept is embodied and human-centered. Classical theism needs divine mind and agency, but God is not a person in the ordinary embodied sense. Theology must therefore use analogy, technical extension, or a doctrine of divine personhood that is not merely anthropomorphic.

The sixth problem is the risk of pantheism or process collapse. Galen’s panpsychism does not entail religion, and a universe-wide subject would not automatically be God. Theological panpsychism must preserve the distinction between created consciousness and divine consciousness.

Conclusion

The distilled Strawsonian doctrine is this: mind is not a ghostly substance hidden behind the body, and consciousness is not an illusion generated by matter. Persons are the primary public bearers of mental and bodily predicates; experience is an irreducible concrete reality; physicalism, if realistic, must include experience in the intrinsic nature of the physical; the self is both a thick embodied person and, at the experiential limit, a thin subject of consciousness; and the soul, if the term is retained, should be understood as the embodied person’s irreducible conscious and moral life rather than as a detachable Cartesian ego.

Theologically, this is a demanding but fruitful picture. It weakens crude soul-body dualism, strengthens embodied and hylomorphic anthropologies, gives resurrection more philosophical dignity than disembodied survival, permits careful forms of theological panpsychism, and requires a refined account of divine personhood. It also makes consciousness central to creation rather than a late accidental anomaly. The mind is not outside nature; nature, rightly understood, was never mindless in the first place.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
